dcterms_abstract: Proof copies of books are often issued in anticipation of official publication for the purposes of editing and promotion. Despite the stated fact of its being an “Uncorrected Proof,” this unique copy of A Gentle Madness bears extensive corrections by the author as he made the final preparations for the text’s publication.

dcterms_abstract: This report, prepared in the early months of writing A Gentle Madness, offers an intimate portrait of the author at work, capitalizing upon his instincts as a journalist and the natural inclinations of his subjects to discuss the esoteric world of the book trade. As Basbanes notes here, “Book people love to talk.” Among the luminaries he interviewed in these months were L.D. Brodsky, Leonard Baskin, William Reese, Walter Pforzheimer -- all titans of the book world. The anecdotes he collected would serve as springboards for his sustained examination of book collecting. He writes, “Though A Gentle Madness is not a how-to guide, we will learn a lot about how these people buy what they buy.”
dc_title: Status Report, 1989 for A Gentle Madness

dcterms_abstract: In January 1991, Basbanes traveled to Des Moines, Iowa, to attend the trial of Stephen Blumberg, who stood accused of stealing nearly 24,000 books from cultural institutions across the United States and Canada. During this time, Basbanes had an exceptional opportunity to travel with Blumberg to his home in Ottumwa, Iowa. The town is notable as being nearly in the center of the country, thus making it the ideal base of operations for Blumberg as he drove around the country illicitly acquiring his collection. On the occasion of this, his final visit to the house before being convicted and sentenced, Blumberg offered Basbanes this linocut print -- an original, hand-printed design that demonstrates the biblioklept’s passion for Victorian architecture.
dc_title: Linocut print inscribed to Nicholas A Basbanes

dcterms_abstract: Following the successful conviction of Stephen C. Blumberg, work continued for years to identify and repatriate the stolen goods to their proper institutional homes. In early 1993, nearly 1000 items that remained unclaimed were bestowed to Creighton University, whose personnel were essential to the success of this repatriation effort, which came to be known as the “Omaha Project.” The books were subsequently sold to a board member of the Friends of the Omaha Public Library, who then proceeded to sell them piecemeal. Eventually, a deal was struck whereby the remaining stock was gathered to be sold at the public auction advertised here “with Proof of Clear Title.” The title for the auction comes, of course, from Basbanes’s book, which was by that time already a classic.
dc_title: Cover of "Remnants of A Gentle Madness: The Stephen C Blumberg "Library" of Americana Auction Catalogue "

dcterms_abstract: As an experienced journalist, Basbanes was not often without a pencil and reporter’s notebook. His archives contain scores of scribbled pads like this one, which recounts his fall meeting with Roger Stoddard, Curator of Rare Books for the Houghton Library at Harvard University. As these notes reflect, the conversation ranged from the correct pronunciation of incunable to the collecting habits of Henry Clay Folger and Haven O’More. Virtually all of Basbanes's interviews were also documented using his pocket microcassette recorder. These recordings have now all been digitized by Texas A&M University Libraries to ensure their continued accessibility for future research.
dc_title: Interview notes for A Gentle Madness

dcterms_abstract: Following the sale of the Garden Ltd. collection in the fall of 1989, Basbanes began to investigate the man who called himself “The World’s Greatest Book Collector.” Having heard that the auction was sparked by a disagreement between the two principles in the Garden Ltd., he travelled to Middlesex County Superior Court and requested the docket sheet for Davis vs. O’More (Case No. 88-635). Following his instincts as an investigative journalist, Basbanes immediately ordered a copy be made in order to secure his source. He then asked to see the case file, which was refused despite its being a public document. His subsequent formal written requests to see the file were also denied. Upon later returning to the court, Basbanes found that even the docket sheet had been impounded since he began his investigation. Nevertheless, he was able to glean important details from his photocopy of the docket sheet and would later refer to it as his “Rosetta Stone.”
dc_title: Letter to the Hon Arthur M Mason from Nicholas A Basbanes, December 3, 1989
dc_subject: Basbanes, Nicholas A., 1943-|Trials|O'More, Haven|Book collectors|Mason, Arthur M.|Letters|Garden Ltd.

dcterms_abstract: The bookplates collected here were removed by Stephen Blumberg from volumes he stole and arranged geographically into these blank pages. In addition to being trophies of his many exploits, however, the bookplates provided valuable documentary evidence to suggest whence the books had been stolen. The present volume is one of three such scrapbooks, which were presented to Basbanes following the end of the trial. Ironically, many personal bookplates bear warnings against book-theft and levy curses upon those who borrow but do not return.
dc_title: Scrapbook of Stolen Bookplates. Volume Three

dcterms_abstract: The bookplates collected here were removed by Stephen Blumberg from volumes he stole and arranged geographically into these blank pages. In addition to being trophies of his many exploits, however, the bookplates provided valuable documentary evidence to suggest whence the books had been stolen. The present volume is one of three such scrapbooks, which were presented to Basbanes following the end of the trial. Ironically, many personal bookplates bear warnings against book-theft and levy curses upon those who borrow but do not return.  Alongside the bookplates themselves, tongue-in-cheek additions such as a notice of the California penal code related to the defacement of books offer a rare personal window into the mind of the biblioklept. A clipping adhered to the front pastedown reads: "A hungry man who steals a piece of beef/To save his life is called a common thief./A thief much commoner we often find/Who robs our bookshelf to improve his mind./Improve your morals, inconsiderate elves,/And then your minds will soon improve themselves."
dc_title: Scrapbook of Stolen Bookplates. Volume One

dcterms_abstract: The case of Stephen Blumberg is the only instance in which bibliokleptomania was defended in an American court with a plea of not guilty by reason of insanity. This transcript documents a conversation between Basbanes and Raymond Cornell, a private investigator tasked with assisting attorney Donald Nickerson in mounting Blumberg’s defense. It chronicles an extraordinary moment in an already sensational story, when the thief was permitted to see his collection a final time. As Cornell recounts, the experience stirred a visceral reaction within him.
dc_title: Transcript of telephone interview with Raymond Cornell, conducted by Nicholas A Basbanes, 30 June 1992

dcterms_abstract: Just weeks after his article was published in Bostonia, Basbanes pitched an expanded book-length treatment of the world of book collecting to Marc Jaffee, an editor at Houghton Mifflin. Included with the proposal is the author’s first outline for the book’s structure. Although the book’s contents would continue to develop in the coming years, Basbanes had already identified a number of important figures he would chronicle, such as the “Giants” (Morgan, Huntington, Folger, et al.) and the “Contemporaries” (McMurtry, Brodsky, and Burden among them). Houghton Mifflin declined to publish the book in May 1989, but Jaffe’s initial encouragement was instrumental to the development of what Basbanes would elsewhere characterize as “a magazine article and an idea” into the international bestseller we celebrate in this exhibition.
dc_title: Early Proposal for Marc Jaffee, 1988

dcterms_abstract: The initial manuscript for A Gentle Madness was completed in the fall of 1991, and was contracted to be published by Random House shortly thereafter. When the firm found itself in dire fiscal straits in early 1992, however, Basbanes received a letter from senior editor Joe Fox, notifying him that the book was to be dropped. In relating this news, Fox cites Basbanes’s indefatigable thoroughness as working both for and against him. In this draft of his reply, Basbanes cites the enduring words of the renowned book collector Henry Huntington: “Men may come and men may go, but books go on forever.” As history would bear out, it was the very tenacity Fox criticized that would endear the author and his books to readers around the world.
dc_title: Copy of Letter to Joseph Fox, January 8, 1993
dc_subject: Basbanes, Nicholas A., 1943-|Gentle madness (Basbanes, Nicholas A., 1943- )|Fox, Joseph|Letters

dcterms_abstract: When they briefly met during the Sotheby’s auction preview in late 1989, Haven O’More expressed some openness to speaking with Basbanes, who was at that time still gathering material for A Gentle Madness. However, repeated attempts to contact O’More via telephone and correspondence proved unsuccessful. The author’s documentation of these attempts provided essential evidence of due diligence when he was sued by O’More for defamation and libel.

dcterms_abstract: “Why is book collecting so compelling and so important?” This deceptively simple question, left in the margins of this revised prospectus for A Gentle Madness by an unnamed reader, gets to the core of the book’s subject. It would prove to be a refrain Basbanes encountered as he pitched the book to agents and editors in the quest to see the book in print.
